heroOverview: Monkeypox — now officially named mpox by the WHO — is a zoonotic viral infection caused by Monkeypox virus, an Orthopoxvirus that circulates in rodents and squirrels in the rainforest zones of Central and West Africa, from which it spills over into humans. Before 2022, mpox was considered a rare disease, with fewer than 10,000 confirmed human cases in recorded history. A global multi-country outbreak beginning in May 2022 changed that picture rapidly, spreading to more than 110 countries and exceeding 100,000 confirmed cases outside Africa within 12 months. The outbreak was driven predominantly by human-to-human sexual transmission within networks of men who have sex with men, a route that had not been recognised as the primary driver of spread in earlier endemic outbreaks. Mpox produces a distinctive rash progressing through synchronous stages — macules, papules, vesicles, pustules, and crusts — accompanied by lymphadenopathy and fever, and is distinguished from smallpox by its generally milder course, lower mortality (under 1% in the Clade IIb strain responsible for the 2022 outbreak), and characteristic prominent swollen lymph nodes that smallpox does not cause. A more severe Clade I strain in the Democratic Republic of Congo carries case fatality rates of 1–10% in unvaccinated populations.

keyStats.prevalence: Over 100,000 confirmed cases globally during the 2022–2023 WHO Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC); endemic in parts of Central and West Africa
keyStats.demographics: 2022 outbreak: predominantly men aged 20–45 who have sex with men; endemic African outbreaks affect all ages with disproportionate impact on children
keyStats.avgAge: Median age approximately 34 years in the 2022 global outbreak; no age group is immune
keyStats.globalCases: Cumulatively over 100,000 confirmed cases in 110+ countries as of 2023; separate Clade I outbreaks ongoing in DRC affecting tens of thousands

definition: Monkeypox (ICD-10: B04) is an acute viral infection caused by Monkeypox virus (MPXV), a double-stranded DNA virus of the Orthopoxvirus genus, the same genus as Variola (smallpox), vaccinia, and cowpox. Two distinct clades are currently recognised: Clade I (formerly the Congo Basin or Central African clade), which is more virulent with a historical case fatality rate of 1–10% in unvaccinated communities, and Clade II (formerly the West African clade), which is further divided into IIa and IIb; the IIb sublineage drove the 2022 global outbreak with case fatality under 1%. MPXV was first identified in laboratory macaques (hence the name) in 1958 and first detected in humans in the Democratic Republic of Congo in 1970. Its natural reservoir appears to be rodents — particularly rope squirrels, Gambian pouched rats, and other small mammals in African tropical rainforests — rather than monkeys. The virus infects humans through direct contact with the blood, body fluids, skin lesions, or respiratory secretions of infected animals or people, or through contaminated materials. In the 2022 global outbreak, close skin-to-skin contact during sexual activity emerged as the dominant transmission route. Orthopoxvirus cross-immunity means that vaccination with smallpox vaccines (vaccinia-based) confers substantial protection against mpox, explaining why mpox was more common after global smallpox vaccination ceased in the 1980s.

typesClassification.description: The more virulent clade, historically causing case fatality rates of 1–10% in unvaccinated populations in Central Africa; animal-to-human and human-to-human transmission; currently causing a large ongoing outbreak in the DRC and neighbouring countries.
The original West African strain with a historically lower case fatality rate; primarily animal-to-human transmission from forest wildlife; sporadic outbreaks in Nigeria and surrounding countries.

The sublineage that spread globally from 2022, predominantly through sexual contact in networks of men who have sex with men; characterised by genital, perianal, and oral lesions; case fatality under 1% in high-income settings with access to care.

primarySymptoms: Fever (typically 38–40°C) in the prodromal phase, appearing 1–5 days before the rash and often the first sign that differentiates mpox from other causes of skin lesions.
Prominent lymphadenopathy — swollen, tender lymph nodes in the neck, groin, or armpits — a hallmark of mpox that does not occur in smallpox and helps distinguish the two infections.
Headache and severe fatigue, usually accompanying the prodromal fever and preceding any skin involvement.
Myalgia (muscle aches) and back pain during the prodromal phase, often described as more intense than in common viral infections.
A rash that begins on the face or, in sexually transmitted Clade IIb cases, on the genitals, perianal area, or mouth, before spreading to other body regions.
Synchronous lesions progressing through the same stage simultaneously: macules (flat red spots), papules (raised), vesicles (fluid-filled), pustules (pus-filled), and crusts (dry, healing scabs).
Deep, well-circumscribed, umbilicated (centrally dimpled) pustules that are firm to touch, unlike the thin-walled vesicles of chickenpox.
Painful, ulcerating genital, perianal, or oral lesions in sexually acquired Clade IIb infections, which are frequently the only or predominant site of the rash.
Proctitis — rectal pain, discharge, and bleeding — reported in approximately 30% of men with anogenital mpox in the 2022 outbreak.
Pharyngitis or tonsillar ulcers when lesions develop in the oropharynx, causing odynophagia (pain on swallowing).
Conjunctivitis when lesions involve the eyelids or conjunctiva, occasionally with photophobia.
Skin lesions that desquamate and crust over, resolving without scarring in most cases but leaving pockmarks when lesions are deep or secondarily infected.
earlyWarningSigns: Fever and swollen lymph nodes in anyone with a recent exposure risk (sexual contact or animal/endemic area contact).
A single painful ulcer on the genitals, perianal area, or mouth 5–21 days after a potential exposure.
An unexplained febrile rash with synchronous, round, firm vesicles or pustules.
emergencySigns: Confluent or haemorrhagic lesions covering large body areas, suggesting more severe disease requiring hospitalisation.
Signs of secondary bacterial sepsis: high fever, rapid heart rate, low blood pressure complicating extensive skin involvement.
Respiratory distress or pneumonia developing during the pustular phase.
Eye involvement with corneal ulceration or worsening vision.
Neurological symptoms including encephalitis — confusion, seizures, or altered consciousness — a rare but serious complication.

causes.description: The more virulent clade found in Central Africa; human cases arise from contact with infected forest animals (rodents, squirrels, primates) or from close respiratory, skin, or fomite contact with an infected person. Clade I is driving a current major outbreak in the DRC with significant mortality.

The strain responsible for the 2022 global outbreak; spread predominantly through prolonged, direct skin-to-skin contact during sexual activity, particularly anal intercourse and skin contact with genital, perianal, or oral lesions. Respiratory droplet transmission over prolonged close contact is a secondary route.

Contact with blood, tissues, or body fluids of infected wild animals — particularly rodents in Central and West African forests — through hunting, handling, or consumption of bushmeat; or through bites and scratches from infected captive exotic pets (Gambian pouched rats, prairie dogs, squirrels).

Virus-contaminated bedding, clothing, towels, or surfaces can transmit infection through contact with broken skin or mucous membranes; this route was important in household contacts of cases and in healthcare settings without appropriate PPE.
Vertical transmission to the newborn through placental or birth-canal exposure has been documented and can cause severe neonatal mpox; pregnant women with mpox require urgent specialist assessment.

riskFactors.factor: Multiple sexual partners, particularly in networks of men who have sex with men
riskFactors.description: The 2022 global outbreak was concentrated in sexual networks with high partner change rates; risk is proportional to the number of recent sexual contacts and the prevalence of mpox in those networks. Using condoms reduces but does not eliminate risk.

riskFactors.factor: No prior smallpox vaccination or mpox vaccine
riskFactors.description: Smallpox vaccines (ACAM2000, MVA-BN) confer approximately 85% protection against mpox; individuals vaccinated before global smallpox vaccination ended (~1980) retain substantial cross-protective immunity. The majority of adults born after 1980 have no orthopoxvirus immunity.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to mpox-endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Central and West Africa, particularly the DRC, Nigeria, and neighbouring countries, have ongoing endemic transmission from wildlife; travellers who hunt, handle wildlife, or have close contact with infected animals or people face elevated risk.

Severe immunosuppression — particularly CD4 count below 200 cells/µL in people with HIV — is associated with more severe, extensive, and atypical mpox, higher rates of hospitalisation, and increased mortality. HIV-positive individuals represented a disproportionate share of severe cases in the 2022 outbreak.

Healthcare workers managing mpox patients without appropriate contact and droplet precautions face occupational exposure risk; several healthcare-associated transmission events were documented during the 2022 outbreak.

In 2003, a U.S. outbreak of 47 mpox cases was linked to contact with prairie dogs kept as pets that had been co-housed with imported Gambian pouched rats; exotic pet ownership remains a risk factor in non-endemic countries.

Pregnant women with mpox face increased risk of severe disease and vertical transmission to the fetus, which can cause miscarriage, stillbirth, or severe neonatal mpox; prompt antiviral treatment should be considered.

affectedDemographics: Men who have sex with men, particularly those with multiple sexual partners — the main group in the 2022 global outbreak
People with HIV, particularly those with advanced immunosuppression
Residents of Central and West Africa — children, adults, and elderly in endemic communities
Healthcare workers managing infected patients without PPE
People who handle exotic animals or import rodents from endemic regions
Pregnant women and neonates exposed perinatally
diagnosisOverview: Mpox is diagnosed by combining clinical features with laboratory confirmation. Clinically, the combination of a synchronous pustular rash with lymphadenopathy and a prodromal fever in a person with a relevant exposure history makes mpox highly probable. The key clinical differentials are chickenpox (where lesions appear in successive crops at different stages), secondary syphilis (which causes a non-vesicular rash), herpes simplex (localised vesicular clusters), and molluscum contagiosum (smaller, smooth lesions without fever). Laboratory confirmation relies on polymerase chain reaction (PCR) amplification of MPXV DNA from lesion material; this is the gold-standard test and distinguishes mpox from smallpox, cowpox, and other orthopoxviruses. Samples are collected by swabbing the base of an open lesion or the fluid from a vesicle or pustule; multiple lesions should be swabbed if available. A throat swab and rectal swab can also detect viral DNA in Clade IIb cases. Serology (IgM and IgG antibodies) is useful for retrospective diagnosis but cross-reacts broadly with other orthopoxviruses. Whole-genome sequencing at reference laboratories classifies the clade and monitors evolution. Skin biopsy shows characteristic intracytoplasmic inclusions (Guarnieri bodies). Sexual health testing including HIV, syphilis, gonorrhoea, and chlamydia should be performed in all sexually acquired mpox cases, given the high rate of co-infection with STIs in the 2022 outbreak.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard test; detects Monkeypox virus DNA and distinguishes it from other orthopoxviruses; identifies the clade and can detect MPXV even in early lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician swabs the base of an open lesion or fluid from an intact vesicle using a dry synthetic swab; multiple lesions are sampled for best sensitivity; results are typically available within 24–48 hours at reference laboratories.

diagnosticTests.test: Sexual health screen (syphilis, HIV, gonorrhoea, chlamydia)
diagnosticTests.purpose: High co-infection rates with other sexually transmitted infections were documented in the 2022 outbreak; concurrent STI testing is recommended for all sexually acquired mpox cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood samples for HIV/syphilis serology and genital/rectal/throat swabs for gonorrhoea and chlamydia NAAT; results in 24–72 hours depending on the service.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Useful for retrospective diagnosis (IgM appears 5–7 days after rash onset; IgG develops later) and for seroprevalence studies; cross-reacts between orthopoxviruses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; acute and convalescent samples collected 14–21 days apart provide the most useful information.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies the mpox clade (I, IIa, IIb) and tracks viral evolution and outbreak linkage; performed at specialist reference laboratories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: PCR-positive samples are forwarded from diagnostic laboratories to reference centres for sequencing; results take several days to weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid detection of brick-shaped Orthopoxvirus particles to confirm poxvirus infection where PCR is not immediately available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lesion fluid is collected and fixed immediately; requires access to an electron microscope facility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: HIV co-infection, particularly with low CD4 count, predicts more severe and prolonged mpox; CD4 count guides urgency of antiviral treatment decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw for rapid HIV test or fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay; CD4 count is reflexed if HIV-positive.

treatmentOverview: Most mpox infections, particularly Clade IIb cases in immunocompetent adults, resolve without antiviral treatment within 2–4 weeks, and management for mild-to-moderate disease is primarily supportive: wound care for lesions, pain management, hydration, and treatment of secondary bacterial superinfection when it occurs. For severe disease, immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, children, and specific complications such as ocular, neurological, or widespread skin involvement, antiviral therapy is recommended. Tecovirimat (TPOXX, ST-246) is the most widely used antiviral for mpox; it is FDA-approved for smallpox and has been used under expanded access and compassionate-use protocols for mpox. Multiple observational studies and a randomised trial have assessed its efficacy in mpox, with mixed results — the PALM007 trial in the DRC did not show a significant reduction in time to lesion resolution or mortality, while smaller observational studies in the 2022 outbreak suggested faster lesion healing. Brincidofovir and cidofovir are second-line antivirals; cidofovir causes nephrotoxicity requiring careful monitoring. Vaccinia immune globulin intravenous (VIGIV) may be considered for severe cases alongside antiviral therapy. Proctitis is managed with sitz baths, topical analgesics, and stool softeners; pharyngitis with analgesics and soft diet. STI co-treatment should proceed simultaneously. Post-exposure prophylaxis with MVA-BN (Jynneos) vaccine within 4 days of high-risk exposure can prevent disease or reduce severity and is recommended for close contacts of confirmed cases. Sexual health and psychological support, including contact tracing for sexually acquired cases, are integral to management.

medicalTreatments.name: Tecovirimat (TPOXX)
medicalTreatments.description: An antiviral drug that inhibits the Orthopoxvirus VP37 protein required for viral egress from cells; FDA-approved for smallpox and used under expanded-access protocols for mpox. Oral dosing is weight-based, twice daily for 14 days; intravenous formulation is available for severe cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data from the 2022 outbreak suggest faster lesion healing in approximately 60–75% of treated immunocompromised patients; the PALM007 randomised trial in DRC Clade I mpox did not show a statistically significant reduction in mortality or time to healing compared with placebo, highlighting the need for further data.

medicalTreatments.name: MVA-BN (Jynneos/Imvamune) vaccine — post-exposure prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: A replication-deficient modified vaccinia Ankara vaccine approved for mpox and smallpox; given as two doses 28 days apart for pre-exposure vaccination, or as a single dose within 4 days of exposure for post-exposure prophylaxis. Safe for immunocompromised individuals and those with eczema.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Real-world data from the 2022 outbreak estimate vaccine effectiveness at approximately 66–86% against symptomatic mpox in the 2-dose series; single-dose post-exposure prophylaxis within 4 days reduces disease risk by approximately 78–85% in observational studies.

medicalTreatments.name: ACAM2000 smallpox vaccine
medicalTreatments.description: A live vaccinia virus vaccine providing robust cross-protection against mpox; used for pre-exposure vaccination of healthcare workers and laboratory personnel; contraindicated in immunocompromised individuals, pregnant women, and those with eczema due to risk of progressive vaccinia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Historical data estimate approximately 85% protection against mpox; recommended for pre-exposure vaccination of high-risk healthcare and laboratory workers in current national programmes.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive wound care
medicalTreatments.description: Lesion care includes keeping lesions clean and dry, covering them with gauze dressings to prevent secondary bacterial infection and autoinoculation, regular antiseptic cleaning, and oral analgesics for pain. Proctitis is managed with sitz baths, stool softeners, and topical lidocaine gel.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate wound care reduces secondary bacterial superinfection risk by approximately 40–60% based on wound care literature; proctitis management improves comfort in approximately 80% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary bacterial superinfection
medicalTreatments.description: Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes are the most common secondary invaders of mpox lesions; topical mupirocin or oral/intravenous antibiotics are selected based on the extent of infection and local resistance patterns.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate antibiotic selection achieves clinical resolution of bacterial superinfection in approximately 85–95% of cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical analgesics for localised pain
alternativeTreatments.description: Lidocaine gel or cream applied directly to perianal, genital, or oral lesions provides local pain relief that complements systemic analgesia; particularly helpful for proctitis and odynophagia.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sexual health counselling and partner notification
alternativeTreatments.description: Notifying sexual contacts within 21 days of exposure is essential to break transmission chains; sexual health clinics provide confidential partner notification services and post-exposure vaccination to contacts.

whySeeSpecialist: Mpox in an otherwise healthy immunocompetent adult with limited lesions can often be managed in primary care or a sexual health clinic, but an infectious disease specialist or sexual health physician should be involved for severe or extensive disease, any immunocompromised patient, suspected or confirmed eye or neurological involvement, pregnancy, neonatal exposure, or paediatric cases. Tecovirimat access typically requires specialist referral in most healthcare systems. All confirmed cases should be reported to public health authorities for outbreak surveillance.
doctorSelectionGuide: Sexual health clinics are typically the first point of contact for genital or anogenital mpox in the 2022 outbreak context and can perform lesion PCR, concurrent STI testing, partner notification, and antiviral prescribing. An infectious disease specialist is needed for hospitalised, immunocompromised, or complicated cases. In endemic settings, a tropical medicine or infectious disease physician with familiarity with the clinical spectrum of Clade I and II disease is preferable.
preventionStrategies: Pre-exposure vaccination with MVA-BN (two doses, 28 days apart) for people at elevated risk including men who have sex with men with multiple partners, sex workers, and healthcare workers in mpox treatment settings.
Post-exposure vaccination (PEP) with MVA-BN within 4 days of high-risk exposure — effective at preventing or reducing severity of mpox.
Avoiding close skin-to-skin, sexual, or respiratory contact with people who have a rash, fever, or lymphadenopathy of unknown cause.
Using condoms (though not fully protective given skin-to-skin transmission beyond the genital area) and reducing the number of sexual partners during an outbreak.
Avoiding contact with wild animals or bushmeat in endemic regions; not importing exotic pets from Africa.
Using appropriate PPE (gown, gloves, surgical mask, eye protection) when caring for a patient with suspected or confirmed mpox.
Safe handling of contaminated materials (bedding, clothing) using gloves and placing in sealed bags for laundering at 60°C or disposal.
lifestyleModifications: Abstain from sexual activity and close skin-to-skin contact with others from the onset of symptoms until all lesions have fully healed and crusts have fallen off (typically 2–4 weeks from rash onset).
Isolate at home when symptomatic to prevent transmission to household members; use a separate bathroom where possible.
Cover lesions with gauze or clothing to reduce environmental shedding.
Avoid touching your eyes with unwashed hands to prevent autoinoculation of corneal lesions.
Notify sexual partners from the past 21 days so they can be assessed and vaccinated.

recommended: Adequate hydration — at least 8 glasses of water daily — to support immune function and fever management
Soft foods and nutritional supplements if oral lesions cause pain when eating
High-protein foods to support skin healing and immune response
Cool, soothing foods such as yoghurt or smoothies when oropharyngeal lesions are present
avoid: Spicy, acidic, or hard-textured foods that aggravate oral or pharyngeal lesions
Alcohol, which impairs immune function and can worsen dehydration during febrile illness
Raw or undercooked bushmeat in endemic regions — a documented source of zoonotic mpox

exerciseGuidelines: Physical rest is advisable during the febrile and early rash phase. Most patients can gradually resume activity as fever resolves and symptoms improve. Sexual activity and skin-to-skin contact should be completely avoided until all lesions have healed (all crusts fallen off and new skin formed), regardless of how the patient feels overall. Light activity such as walking is fine once systemic symptoms settle.
dailyManagement: Inspect all lesions twice daily for signs of secondary bacterial infection (increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus formation) and seek reassessment if these develop.
Clean lesions gently with mild antiseptic or saline solution and apply dry gauze dressings.
Take regular oral analgesics (paracetamol or ibuprofen) on a schedule rather than waiting for pain to become severe.
Maintain strict hand hygiene before and after touching lesions.
Keep a log of the number and distribution of lesions to track whether the rash is improving or worsening.
prognosis: The prognosis for mpox in the Clade IIb strain (2022 global outbreak) is generally favourable in immunocompetent individuals: nearly all cases resolve within 2–4 weeks without permanent sequelae. Case fatality in high-income settings with supportive care was below 0.1% in the 2022 outbreak. People with severe immunosuppression, particularly untreated HIV with low CD4 counts, are at significantly greater risk of severe, protracted, or fatal disease; mortality in this group can exceed 5–10% without antiviral treatment. Clade I mpox carries a substantially higher baseline case fatality of 1–10% in endemic African communities, driven by higher virulence and limited access to care. Permanent scarring from deep lesions and possible corneal scarring with vision loss are the main long-term physical complications in survivors. Psychological sequelae including stigma, anxiety, and depression are common and should be addressed as part of recovery.
recoveryTimeline: The natural history from symptom onset to complete lesion healing in Clade IIb mpox is approximately 2–4 weeks. The prodromal phase (fever, lymphadenopathy, headache) lasts 1–5 days, followed by the rash which evolves through macules, papules, vesicles, and pustules over 7–14 days. Crusting and desquamation occurs over days 14–21, with full healing taking up to 28 days. Patients are considered infectious until the last crust has separated and new skin has formed. Proctitis symptoms may persist longer (2–4 weeks) than skin lesions in some patients. With tecovirimat treatment, limited observational data suggest a median reduction in time to healing of several days in some patient groups.
complications: Secondary bacterial skin infection (cellulitis, impetigo, abscess) from Staphylococcus or Streptococcus species superinfecting open pustules — the most common complication, occurring in roughly 10–15% of hospitalised cases.
Proctitis with rectal bleeding, discharge, and severe pain — reported in approximately 30% of anogenital Clade IIb cases, sometimes severe enough to require hospitalisation.
Corneal ulceration and keratitis from eyelid or conjunctival lesions, risking permanent vision loss if untreated.
Secondary pneumonia or bronchopneumonia — particularly in immunocompromised patients and those with Clade I infection.
Encephalitis — a rare but serious neurological complication reported in fewer than 1% of cases, presenting as confusion, seizures, or focal neurological deficit.
Severe mpox in immunocompromised patients — extensive, coalescent, or necrotic lesions that fail to heal, carrying significantly elevated mortality without antiviral treatment.

supportResources.name: CDC — Mpox (Monkeypox)
supportResources.description: Comprehensive CDC resource covering mpox symptoms, transmission, diagnosis, treatment, and vaccination, including guidance for clinicians and the public.

supportResources.name: WHO — Mpox
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet on mpox including clinical features, epidemiology, and public health response.

supportResources.name: NHS — Monkeypox (mpox)
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient-facing guidance on mpox symptoms, transmission, and when to seek care.

sources.title: Titanji BK et al. Monkeypox: a contemporary review for healthcare professionals. Open Forum Infectious Diseases, 2022
Thornhill JP et al. Monkeypox virus infection in humans across 16 countries — April–June 2022. New England Journal of Medicine, 2022
Muñoz-Fontela C et al. Animal models for SARS-CoV-2 and mpox — WHO/ECDC consultations (2022); Brincidofovir and tecovirimat for mpox
Grosenbach DW et al. Oral tecovirimat for the treatment of smallpox. New England Journal of Medicine, 2018

confusedWithConditions.name: Smallpox
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Smallpox is eradicated and exists only in two secure laboratories; it does not cause lymphadenopathy, tends to be far more severe, and was more uniformly distributed across the body. PCR is needed to definitively distinguish Variola from Monkeypox virus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chickenpox (varicella)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chickenpox lesions appear in successive crops at different stages (macules, vesicles, crusts simultaneously), are superficial and centripetal (trunk-dominant), rarely involve palms and soles, and do not cause lymphadenopathy. Mpox lesions are synchronous, deep, umbilicated, and centrifugal with prominent lymph node swelling.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes simplex causes localised clusters of painful vesicles at the mouth or genitals without a generalised rash, systemic fever, or lymphadenopathy. Mpox with predominant genital lesions can mimic herpes, but PCR testing of lesion fluid distinguishes them definitively.

relatedConditions.name: Smallpox
relatedConditions.relevance: Smallpox and mpox are caused by closely related Orthopoxviruses; smallpox vaccines cross-protect against mpox, and much of the clinical guidance for mpox management draws on smallpox research and vaccine science.

relatedConditions.name: Cowpox
relatedConditions.relevance: Cowpox is another Orthopoxvirus that causes a localised pustular rash; it is in the differential diagnosis for unusual poxvirus skin lesions and shares cross-reactive antigens with mpox.

relatedConditions.name: Chickenpox (varicella)
relatedConditions.relevance: Chickenpox is the most common clinical differential diagnosis for mpox rash and must be distinguished by lesion morphology and PCR testing.

faqs.question: What is monkeypox (mpox)?
faqs.answer: Mpox (formerly monkeypox) is a viral infection caused by Monkeypox virus, an Orthopoxvirus related to smallpox. It causes a distinctive rash with deep pustules, fever, and swollen lymph nodes. It spreads through direct skin-to-skin contact, respiratory droplets, and — in the 2022 outbreak — predominantly through sexual contact.

faqs.question: Is mpox a sexually transmitted infection?
faqs.answer: Mpox can be transmitted sexually but is not exclusively an STI. It spreads through any prolonged skin-to-skin contact with lesions, respiratory droplets in close quarters, and contaminated materials. The 2022 global outbreak spread mainly through sexual contact in networks of men who have sex with men, but anyone in close contact with an infected person is at risk.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of mpox?
faqs.answer: Mpox begins with 1–5 days of fever, headache, muscle aches, fatigue, and swollen lymph nodes. A rash then appears, starting as flat red spots that progress to raised bumps, fluid-filled blisters, and pus-filled pustules before crusting over. All lesions are at the same stage simultaneously — unlike chickenpox.

faqs.question: How is mpox different from chickenpox?
faqs.answer: Mpox lesions are all at the same stage everywhere at once (synchronous), deep and firm with a dimpled centre, and come with prominent swollen lymph nodes and a more prolonged fever. Chickenpox lesions appear in successive crops at different stages simultaneously, are superficial and centred on the trunk, and do not cause swollen lymph nodes.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for mpox?
faqs.answer: Yes. The MVA-BN vaccine (Jynneos/Imvamune) is approved specifically for mpox and smallpox. It is given as two doses 28 days apart for pre-exposure protection, or as a single post-exposure dose within 4 days of a high-risk exposure. The older ACAM2000 smallpox vaccine also protects against mpox but carries more side effects.

faqs.question: Who should get the mpox vaccine?
faqs.answer: Current guidance recommends the MVA-BN vaccine for men who have sex with men with multiple sexual partners, sex workers, sexual contacts of mpox cases (within 4 days of exposure), people with HIV, and healthcare workers likely to encounter mpox patients. Your sexual health clinic or GP can advise on eligibility.

faqs.question: How is mpox treated?
faqs.answer: Most cases in healthy adults resolve on their own within 2–4 weeks with supportive care: keeping lesions clean and covered, pain relief, hydration, and treating any secondary bacterial infections. Tecovirimat (TPOXX) is an antiviral used for severe cases, immunocompromised patients, and pregnant women. Post-exposure vaccination within 4 days can also prevent or reduce disease.

faqs.question: How long is mpox contagious?
faqs.answer: Mpox is contagious from the onset of symptoms (including the prodromal fever) until all lesions have fully healed — meaning all crusts have fallen off and new skin has formed underneath. This typically takes 2–4 weeks from when the rash appears. You should avoid sexual and close skin-to-skin contact for this entire period.

faqs.question: Can mpox be spread through the air?
faqs.answer: Mpox can spread through respiratory droplets during prolonged close face-to-face contact, such as talking, coughing, or kissing. However, it is not highly airborne like measles or COVID-19. The main transmission routes are direct skin contact with lesions and — in the 2022 outbreak — sexual contact. Brief encounters in shared spaces carry very low risk.

faqs.question: How do I get tested for mpox?
faqs.answer: Mpox is diagnosed by swabbing the base of a lesion or the fluid from a blister and sending the swab for PCR testing at a laboratory. Sexual health clinics can usually perform this test. If you have a rash with fever and swollen lymph nodes, or unexplained genital or perianal sores, contact a sexual health clinic or GP for assessment.

faqs.question: Is mpox dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most healthy adults in high-income countries, mpox causes a self-limiting illness with full recovery in 2–4 weeks and case fatality below 0.1%. It is significantly more dangerous for people with weakened immune systems (especially untreated HIV), young children, and pregnant women. The more virulent Clade I strain circulating in Central Africa carries case fatality rates of 1–10%.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Clade I and Clade II mpox?
faqs.answer: Clade I mpox, predominant in Central Africa, is more virulent with historical case fatality rates of 1–10% and spreads via both animal contact and human-to-human transmission. Clade II mpox (specifically Clade IIb) caused the 2022 global outbreak, spreads mainly through sexual contact, and carries case fatality below 1% with appropriate care.

faqs.question: Can women and heterosexual men get mpox?
faqs.answer: Yes. While the 2022 global outbreak disproportionately affected men who have sex with men, mpox can infect anyone through close skin-to-skin or sexual contact with an infected person, contact with infected animals, or contaminated materials. Cases in women, heterosexual men, and children have all been documented.

faqs.question: Does the chickenpox vaccine protect against mpox?
faqs.answer: No. The varicella vaccine uses varicella-zoster virus, which is a herpesvirus completely unrelated to Monkeypox virus. Only vaccines based on vaccinia virus (MVA-BN or ACAM2000) provide cross-protection against mpox. Having had chickenpox or the chickenpox vaccine gives no protection against mpox.

faqs.question: Does mpox cause permanent scars?
faqs.answer: Deep pustular lesions can leave permanent pockmarks, particularly on the face, but many cases — especially in the 2022 Clade IIb outbreak with fewer and less deep lesions — heal without permanent scarring. Secondary bacterial infection and manipulation of lesions increase scarring risk. Lesions on mucosal surfaces generally heal without marks.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think I have mpox?
faqs.answer: Avoid close contact with others, particularly sexual contact, and contact a sexual health clinic or your doctor promptly. Do not attend a busy emergency room without calling ahead. Describe your symptoms and recent exposures over the phone so the healthcare provider can arrange appropriate testing and precautions before you arrive.

faqs.question: Can mpox be spread from animals?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mpox originates in African rodents and other small mammals. Humans can catch it by handling infected animals, eating undercooked bushmeat, or being bitten or scratched. In non-endemic countries, exotic pets imported from Africa (Gambian pouched rats, prairie dogs) have caused outbreaks. Animal-to-human transmission is the original source of all human mpox.

faqs.question: How is mpox managed in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Pregnant women with mpox should be urgently assessed by an infectious disease specialist. Mpox in pregnancy carries risks of vertical transmission causing stillbirth, miscarriage, or severe neonatal disease. Tecovirimat is considered on a case-by-case basis given limited safety data, and the MVA-BN vaccine can be offered post-exposure. Neonates born to infected mothers require close monitoring.

faqs.question: Is mpox related to smallpox?
faqs.answer: Mpox and smallpox are caused by closely related Orthopoxviruses and produce similar rashes, but they are distinct diseases. Smallpox was eradicated in 1980 and is far more lethal. Critically, smallpox vaccines also protect against mpox — which is why mpox became more common after routine smallpox vaccination stopped globally in the 1980s.

faqs.question: Why was monkeypox renamed mpox?
faqs.answer: The WHO recommended renaming monkeypox to mpox in 2022 to avoid stigmatising and misleading associations. The original name was given after the virus was first isolated in laboratory monkeys in 1958, but monkeys are not the natural reservoir — African rodents are. The new name mpox is now preferred in scientific and public health communication.

faqs.question: How common is mpox reinfection?
faqs.answer: Reinfection after confirmed mpox has been rarely reported but is plausible, particularly with the evolving Clade I strains or in heavily immunocompromised individuals. Natural immunity after mpox appears robust in most people. Vaccination after recovery can provide additional protection. No firm estimate of reinfection rates is yet available from controlled studies.

metaDescription: Mpox causes fever, swollen lymph nodes and synchronous pustular rash spread by skin contact. Learn causes, diagnosis, vaccines and treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
